name: Ireland
description: The western island of the British Isles. Gaelic Celtic homeland, early monastic civilization, later subject to Viking settlement and Norman conquest.

periods.note: Gaelic Ireland — never conquered by Rome. Tribal Gaelic society of five provinces (Ulster, Munster, Leinster, Connacht, Meath) under a high king (Ard Rí) whose authority was contested. Iron Age hillforts, brehon law, and an oral culture sustained by a powerful druidic class.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Q-Celtic Gaelic language and tribal society; no Roman influence

periods.note: Age of Saints — Patrick's mission (432) began rapid Christianisation; Ireland became the Isle of Saints and Scholars while Continental Europe fell to Germanic invasions. Irish monasteries (Iona, Clonmacnoise, Glendalough) preserved Latin learning and spread Christianity to Scotland and Europe. High Kings of Ireland contested: Brian Boru briefly unified Ireland (1002–1014).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — monastic Gaelic civilisation at its cultural peak

periods.note: Viking Age and Gaelic recovery — Norse raids devastated monasteries from 795; Norse settled and founded Dublin, Waterford, Limerick, Wexford as trading towns (900s). Brian Boru defeated a Norse-Leinster alliance at Clontarf (1014) but was killed; high kingship contested again. Gaelic-Norse cultural synthesis in coastal towns.

ethnic_composition.presence: coastal towns — Norse-Gaelic ('Hiberno-Norse') hybrid culture in Dublin and other ports

periods.note: Lordship of Ireland — Norman barons (Strongbow, de Courcy) invaded at a deposed Irish king's invitation 1169; Henry II asserted overlordship 1171. The Normans were never fully able to conquer the island; the Statutes of Kilkenny (1366) tried to prevent the Anglo-Norman settlers from 'going native' (becoming more Irish than the Irish). The Pale — the area around Dublin under effective English control — shrank over time.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant outside the Pale — Gaelic lords retained control of most of Ireland
Anglo-Norman settler class in coastal areas and the Pale; many Hibernicised

periods.note: Kingdom of Ireland — Henry VIII proclaimed himself King of Ireland 1541; Tudor conquest attempted to extend English control over the whole island. Plantation policy settled Protestant English and Scottish colonists on confiscated Gaelic lands (Ulster Plantation 1610). Nine Years' War (1593–1603) was the last great Gaelic resistance. 1641 rebellion; Cromwellian conquest 1649–53 was devastating — land confiscations, mass death, transportation of survivors.

ethnic_composition.presence: majority but land-dispossessed; Catholic; increasingly impoverished under penal laws
settler Protestant landlord class (Church of Ireland)

periods.note: Ireland remained as a separate Kingdom under the British Crown through this period (not part of Great Britain). Protestant Ascendancy dominated. 1798 United Irish Rebellion (inspired by French Revolution); suppressed; used as justification for the Act of Union.

ethnic_composition.presence: large Catholic majority — landless, politically excluded under Penal Laws

periods.note: Act of Union 1800 merged Ireland into the United Kingdom. Catholic Emancipation 1829. Great Famine 1845–52 killed one million and drove two million to emigrate — Ireland's population halved in a decade and never recovered. Rising nationalism; Easter Rising 1916; War of Independence 1919–21; Irish Free State 1922.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant; Irish-language in terminal decline after the Famine; English replaced it

periods.note: Irish Free State (1922) → Republic of Ireland (1949). Northern Ireland (six Ulster counties) remained in the UK. The Troubles (1968–1998) — sectarian conflict between republican (Catholic) and loyalist (Protestant) communities in Northern Ireland. Good Friday Agreement 1998.
